objectives: Perform comprehensive security audit of mx20022 financial message library
Check for OWASP Top 10, unsafe code, XML vulnerabilities, input validation, financial calc risks
constraints: Financial message parsing library (ISO 20022, SWIFT MT)
important_facts: unsafe_code = forbid at workspace level - zero unsafe blocks found
cargo audit reports zero dependency advisories (131 crate deps scanned)
quick-xml 0.37 does not support external entity resolution - safe against XXE by design
Amount validation uses f64 for comparison in SEPA validator (minor precision concern)
FedNow validator correctly uses integer-cent arithmetic for amount comparisons
No input size limits on CLI file reads
Error messages include user-controlled values (potential minor info leak)
Regex pattern compilation from XSD is compile-time only (codegen), not runtime user input
MT parser handles nested braces correctly with depth counting
risks_issues: SEPA f64 amount comparison: could theoretically misclassify boundary amounts
No file size limits in CLI commands: potential OOM with very large files
Validation error messages echo user input values

iteration.summary: Performed comprehensive security audit of all 7 crates in the mx20022 workspace. Examined XML parsing (quick-xml), SWIFT MT parsing, validation rules (IBAN, BIC, LEI, amount, pattern), scheme validators (FedNow, SEPA, CBPR+), CLI input handling, codegen, and dependency chain. No critical vulnerabilities found. The codebase demonstrates strong security posture with workspace-level unsafe forbid, clean cargo audit, and proper input validation. Several low/medium findings documented around f64 arithmetic in SEPA, missing CLI input size limits, and error message information exposure.
next_actions: Fix SEPA f64 amount comparisons to use integer arithmetic like FedNow
Add file size limits to CLI commands
Consider sanitizing user values in error messages for production deployments
